Title: Pathogen-induced pH changes regulate the growth-defense balance in plants

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: COMPANION OF CELLULOSE SYNTHASE protein; microtubule-interacting domain
Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane-associated cellulose synthase complex / cortical microtubule interface
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant biomass production; cell wall structure; growth-related mechanical support
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for Fo5176-induced depletion of cellulose synthase complexes, microtubule depolymerization, pH-dependent root growth inhibition, and full fungal vascular colonization
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cc1cc2 mutant analysis; complementation with GFP-CC1; truncation analysis with GFP-CC1ΔN120; live-cell imaging; pH sensor introgression; AHA phosphorylation assay; infection assay
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CC2; cellulose synthase complex; cortical microtubules; AHA (functional relationship)
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fusarium oxysporum Fo5176 hyphae; fungal elicitors; MES buffering
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of CC1 function is linked in this paper to reduced susceptibility to Fusarium, but discussion notes opposite roles under abiotic stress such as salt
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CC1-related manipulation could reduce Fusarium vascular colonization by altering plasma membrane ΔpH responsiveness, but likely requires caution because CC proteins also mediate abiotic stress responses
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The microtubule-interacting domain of CC1 is specifically required; CC1 acts at the nexus of growth, cellulose synthesis machinery behavior, pH regulation, and defense
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CC1 is necessary for rapid fungal-triggered rearrangement of the CSC-microtubule machinery. 2. CC1 function contributes to regulation of basal and inducible plasma membrane proton pump activity. 3. Loss of CC1/CC2 reduces Fusarium xylem colonization and root growth inhibition.

Common_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plasma membrane-associated cellulose synthase complex / cortical microtubule interface
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant biomass production; cell wall structure; growth-related mechanical support
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Together with CC1, required for fungal-triggered cellulose synthase complex depletion, microtubule changes, proton pump regulation, and normal susceptibility to vascular colonization
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cc1cc2 double-mutant analysis; live-cell imaging; pH measurements; AHA phosphorylation assays; infection assays
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CC1; cellulose synthase complex; cortical microtubules; AHA (functional relationship)
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fusarium oxysporum Fo5176 hyphae; fungal elicitors; MES buffering
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: As part of cc1cc2, reduced Fusarium susceptibility is accompanied by altered cellulose homeostasis and likely abiotic stress liabilities discussed by the authors
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Targeting the CC module may help manipulate growth-defense balance and vascular disease susceptibility, with caution for stress tradeoffs
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The experimental evidence is primarily on the cc1cc2 double mutant, so CC2 is supported as part of the core CC module rather than individually dissected
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CC2 is part of the CC module required for fungal-induced CSC and microtubule responses. 2. Loss of CC1/CC2 causes elevated basal plasma membrane ΔpH and AHA phosphorylation. 3. The cc1cc2 mutant is less susceptible to Fusarium vascular colonization.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Proton transport / plasma membrane pH homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: ADP; inorganic phosphate; apoplastic proton gradient
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes ATP to pump protons from the cortical/cytosolic side across the plasma membrane into the apoplast, generating a proton chemical gradient
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point for plasma membrane ΔpH changes in this study
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Plant biomass production; cell wall structure; growth-related mechanical support
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Fo5176 contact increases AHA phosphorylation/activation, causing rapid apoplastic acidification, reduced cellulose synthase complex activity, reduced root growth, and altered susceptibility to fungal colonization
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Fusarium oxysporum Fo5176 hyphae; fungal elicitor mix; MES buffering
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Anti-pThr/anti-AHA western blotting; apoplastic and cortical pH sensor imaging; media acidification assay; fungal treatment; MES buffering
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Hyperactivation is associated with lower basal cellulose content in cc1cc2 under unbuffered conditions and altered hygromycin sensitivity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Modulating AHA activity or plasma membrane ΔpH may help tune growth-defense balance and reduce Fusarium vascular colonization, but risks altered growth/cellulose traits
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper mainly measures AHA activation state with family-level antibodies and does not resolve a single AHA isoform; effect is localized to the plasma membrane interface
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Fo5176 hyphae rapidly increased phosphorylation of plasma membrane AHAs. 2. AHA activation caused apoplastic acidification and transient cortical pH changes. 3. These pH changes were linked to reduced cellulose synthase machinery at the plasma membrane, reduced root growth, and altered pathogen progression.
